title: Rabbit handling and restraint
subtitle: Safe holds that prevent injury to rabbit and handler.
excerpt: A rabbit that struggles free of a poor hold and drops can break its own back in the fall. Correct handling technique prevents injury, reduces stress, and makes veterinary checks and routine health inspections much faster. The rabbit should be supported under its hindquarters at all times; it should never hang freely from the scruff.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Rabbits (2019). RSPCA rabbit welfare guidance, rspca.org.uk. Cobbett, Cottage Economy (1822), Project Gutenberg, basic handling principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A natural freeze response in prey animals including rabbits, where the animal becomes temporarily motionless when placed on its back. Often misread as relaxation; the rabbit is actually in a state of extreme fear. Handling methods that induce tonic immobility should be avoided.

content.text: A rabbit's skeleton is relatively fragile compared with its powerful hind legs. When a rabbit panics in a poor hold and kicks hard to escape, the sudden force can fracture the lumbar spine, a catastrophic injury in an otherwise healthy animal. Most injuries of this type happen to handlers who pick up a rabbit without supporting its hindquarters.

Approach calmly and scoop the rabbit from below rather than grabbing from above. Place one hand under the rabbit's chest and support its hind legs and rump firmly with the other. Bring the rabbit against your body with the hindquarters tucked under your arm or across your chest, the rabbit's back legs must be constrained, not free to kick.

Hold the rabbit close to your body with its head tucked under your arm and hindquarters supported by your forearm. This position allows you to move freely while keeping both ends of the rabbit constrained. For larger breeds, two-handed support (chest in one hand, rump in the other) is more secure for short distances.

Lower the rabbit hind-first onto a surface that offers grip, not a smooth table. Allow the hind feet to contact the surface before releasing the chest, then support until the rabbit has balanced itself. A rabbit placed too quickly onto a slippery surface will scramble, fall, or leap and land poorly.
Do not lift a rabbit by the ears; this causes pain and tissue damage.
Do not place a rabbit on its back,
looks like relaxation but is a stress response.
Children must not carry rabbits unsupervised. Most rabbit falls involve children.

For mouth checks, eye inspection, and nail trimming, place the rabbit in a low-sided box or on a non-slip mat on a table. A second person holding the rabbit against their body while you examine it is more reliable than working alone. For injections, wrap the rabbit in a towel with only the scruff area exposed, the towel wrap prevents leg kicks without causing distress.
